WarehousePosition.title: WarehousePosition
WarehousePosition.description: Represents a physical or logical structure within the warehouse, such as a rack or a floor block, with its layout and occupancy data.

id.description: Unique identifier for the WarehousePosition entity.

code.description: A human-readable code identifying the warehouse position, e.g., 'Rua-A-01-1'.

type.description: The category of the warehouse structure, such as 'RACK' for vertical shelving or 'FLOOR_BLOCK' for floor-level areas.

position.description: The 3D coordinates [x, y, z] representing the structure's origin in the warehouse layout.

rotation.description: The 3D rotation [x, y, z] of the structure in radians, typically around its local axes.

dimensions.description: The 3D dimensions [width, height, depth] of the structure in units relevant to the 3D scene.

occupancyPercentage.description: The current occupancy level of the position, expressed as a percentage. This can exceed 100% for overloaded positions.

status.description: The operational status of the warehouse position (e.g., 'NORMAL', 'ALERT', 'EMPTY', 'BLOCKED'), indicating its current condition.

lastUpdated.description: Timestamp indicating when the position's data was last updated.

InventoryItem.title: InventoryItem
InventoryItem.description: Represents a specific product or a batch of products stored within a particular warehouse position.

id.description: Unique identifier for the InventoryItem entity.

sku.description: Stock Keeping Unit, a unique identifier for the product type.

description.description: A brief textual description of the product.

quantity.description: The count or amount of the product associated with this inventory item record.

lpn.description: License Plate Number, an optional identifier for a logistics unit (e.g., pallet, container) containing the product.

warehousePositionId.description: Reference to the WarehousePosition where this inventory item is physically located. (Relationship: WarehousePosition 1:N InventoryItem)

definition.description: Collection to store UIDs of users authorized as warehouse managers. Documents are primarily for existence checks for Database-Backed Access Control (DBAC), enabling Authorization Independence for other collections.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier (UID) of the user who is a warehouse manager.

definition.description: Root collection for all warehouse positions. Access is restricted to authorized `warehouseManagers` via DBAC.

params.name: warehousePositionId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific warehouse position.

definition.description: Subcollection containing inventory items for a specific warehouse position. Includes `warehousePositionId` for explicit relational integrity and is secured by the global `warehouseManager` role.

params.name: warehousePositionId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent warehouse position.

params.name: inventoryItemId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific inventory item.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to be secure, scalable, and debuggable, prioritizing Authorization Independence and secure `list` operations (QAPs). Given the application's context as a 'Digital Twin' for 'Warehouse Operations Managers', the core data (`WarehousePosition` and `InventoryItem`) is treated as collaborative data for a team of managers, rather than user-specific private data.

1.  **Global Authorization for Managers (`/warehouseManagers/{userId}`):** To implement Database-Backed Access Control (DBAC) and enable Authorization Independence, a dedicated top-level collection `/warehouseManagers/{userId}` is introduced. This collection's documents act as implicit roles; the *existence* of a document with a user's `uid` here grants them management access to all warehouse data. This adheres to the 'Existence over Content' principle for global roles. By checking `exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/warehouseManagers/$(request.auth.uid))` in Firestore Security Rules, we avoid `get()` calls to parent documents and make authorization atomic and easy to debug. This setup explicitly makes authorization intent clear.

2.  **Top-Level `WarehousePosition` (`/warehousePositions/{warehousePositionId}`):** All `WarehousePosition` documents reside in a single top-level collection. This follows the Structural Segregation mandate as all documents within this collection share the same security posture: they are accessible to any user listed in the `/warehouseManagers` collection. This structure simplifies rules by centralizing the authorization check to the global manager role.

3.  **Nested `InventoryItem` (`/warehousePositions/{warehousePositionId}/inventoryItems/{inventoryItemId}`):** `InventoryItem` documents are stored as a subcollection of their respective `WarehousePosition`. This represents the strong `1:N` hierarchical relationship and continues the path structure for nested data. Each `InventoryItem` document also contains `warehousePositionId` explicitly, which, although redundant with the path, ensures data integrity and supports rule validation (e.g., `request.resource.data.warehousePositionId == warehousePositionId`).

**How Authorization Independence is Achieved:**
*   For `WarehousePosition` documents: Access rules will directly reference the `/warehouseManagers` collection, e.g., `allow read, write: if exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/warehouseManagers/$(request.auth.uid));`. This does not require fetching data from any parent document or from the `WarehousePosition` itself beyond `request.auth.uid`.
*   For `InventoryItem` documents: Access rules will also check the global `/warehouseManagers` collection. For example, `allow read, write: if exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/warehouseManagers/$(request.auth.uid));`. Because the `InventoryItem`'s access is globally tied to the same set of managers as its parent `WarehousePosition`, there is no need to denormalize a `members` map from `WarehousePosition` into each `InventoryItem`. The `warehousePositionId` field within `InventoryItem` ensures that its relationship to its parent is explicit.

**How QAPs (Secure Listing) are Supported:**
*   `list` operations on `/warehousePositions` are secure because the rule `exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/warehouseManagers/$(request.auth.uid))` is applied. This means a user can only `list` the `WarehousePosition` documents if their `uid` is present in the `warehouseManagers` collection.
*   Similarly, `list` operations on `/warehousePositions/{warehousePositionId}/inventoryItems` will also be secured by the `exists` check against the global `warehouseManagers` collection. This prevents unauthorized users from listing inventory items for any position.
